Description:
This lesson introduces learners to the principles and practices of troubleshooting common computer problems affecting both hardware and software systems through a structured approach in identifying issues, analyzing root causes, and applying effective solutions to restore system functionality.


Learning Objectives:

This lesson aims to:

  1. identify common computer hardware problems and their indicators;
  2. analyze and diagnose the root causes of hardware issues using a systematic troubleshooting approach; and
  3. apply appropriate step-by-step troubleshooting procedures to resolve hardware issues safely and effectively.

sections.title: Topic 1: What is Troubleshooting?

sections.caption: Lesson 5 Topic 1 Video
sections.content: https://www.dropbox.com/scl/fi/sxgs0i2zficmcqeah3951/Lesson-5-Topic-1.mp4?rlkey=dpaqqy1kfpjmemlx38nldnugs&st=b2cqmdog&dl=0

Troubleshooting is a systematic process used to identify, analyze, and resolve problems within a computer system. It involves observing symptoms, isolating possible causes, and applying solutions to restore normal operation.[1]

Troubleshooting is essential in Computer Hardware Servicing (CHS) because it allows technicians to efficiently diagnose and fix issues affecting computer performance. Without proper troubleshooting, problems may escalate, causing hardware damage, data loss, or system failure.[2]

During this lesson, we will tackle multiple issues commonly encountered with both the computer’s hardware and software. We will learn what are the indicators of these issues happening, what causes them, and what is the procedure we can do to troubleshoot and fix them. Familiarity with the devices will be needed, but rest assured that since you’ve passed the previous lessons, you already have quite a grasp of the fundamentals of CHS and how we will apply it in troubleshooting.

questions.question: What process is used to identify and fix computer problems step-by-step?

sections.title: Topic 2: Hardware Issues

sections.content:
Hardware refers to the physical components of a computer system that can be seen and touched, such as the motherboard, processor, RAM, storage devices, and peripherals.[3]

Hardware issues are common because physical components are constantly exposed to environmental and usage-related stress. Over time, heat, dust, and electrical fluctuations weaken these components. For example, a dust-filled cooling fan can no longer regulate temperature properly, leading to overheating and system failure. While there are lots of possible reasons for hardware issues to happen, we can generally consolidate them into the following reasons:

  1. Wear and Tear – components degrade over time due to continuous use, eventually leading to failure or reduced performance.
  2. Overheating – excessive heat caused by poor ventilation or dust buildup can damage internal parts and shorten their lifespan.
  3. Power Surges – sudden spikes in electricity can damage sensitive electronic components.
  4. Improper Handling – physical damage from dropping or incorrectly installing components can cause malfunction.
  5. Manufacturing Defects – some hardware may fail early due to production faults.

In CHS, resolving hardware issues involves applying technical knowledge, diagnostic tools, and proper procedures to identify and repair faulty components. It includes inspecting connections, testing parts, replacing defective hardware, and ensuring compatibility.[4]

questions.question: Why are hardware issues common in computers?

sections.title: Topic 3: Common Hardware Issues

sections.content:
a. Computer Does Not Turn On / Boot

Before anything appears on the screen, a computer must first receive and distribute power to its internal components, which starts the moment you press the ‘on’ button of your computer. In case the system fails to perform power delivery during this process, it cannot begin the startup process. A failure to power on is often a symptom of deeper electrical issues within the system. If caused by a faulty PSU, unstable or incorrect voltage may have already affected sensitive components such as the motherboard, CPU, or storage devices. Repeated attempts to power on with unresolved electrical issues can increase the risk of short circuits or further damage to internal components. Over time, unresolved power delivery problems may lead to permanent hardware failure, making recovery more difficult and costly.[4][2]

To properly identify this issue, it is important to observe the visible system’s behavior when attempting to power it on. The following signs help confirm whether the problem is related to power delivery or startup failure:

  1. No lights or indicators – shows that no electrical power is reaching or being distributed in the system.[5]
  2. No fan movement – indicates that internal components are not receiving power to operate.
  3. No sound or response – confirms that the system is not initiating the boot process at all.

Once these symptoms are identified, the next step is to understand what could be causing the issue. These possible reasons are all related to failures in the system’s power flow, either externally or internally, and so must be checked to resolve the issue:

  • Faulty Power Supply Unit (PSU) – As discussed in Lesson 3, the power supply is the main source of power conversion inside the system. In cases when a computer fails to get power, the power supply could possibly be preventing the conversion of electricity into usable power for the system.[4]
  • Loose or disconnected cables – As the computer requires electricity to work, anything that interrupts the flow of electricity into the computer, be it internal or external sources, such as connections inside the motherboard, or from the power outlet.[5]
  • Defective power outlet – A power outlet may also fail to provide electricity to the system if it is not functioning optimally.
  • Motherboard failure – In some cases, the motherboard itself may prevent distribution of power to internal components due to possible damage.

Troubleshooting should begin with simple external checks and progress toward internal diagnostics.[2] This way, we will be able to follow a structured procedure to isolate and resolve the issue at hand.

For a computer that does not turn on, we will be going through the following troubleshooting steps
/uploads/lessons/docx-lesson-5-1776122966460-1.png
sideTexts:
1. Check power source
    1. Plug another device into the same outlet to confirm electricity is available.
    2. If there is no electricity in the current outlet, try to switch outlets; if connected to a power strip or extension cord, try to directly connect to a power outlet if other outlets in the power strip does not let the computer to turn on.[5][2]
  2. Secure all cables
    1. Try to check on the connection from the outlet to the system unit. Ensure all power connections are properly attached to maintain consistent electrical flow. It would be best to also check if there are visible damages along the cables or its plugs.[5]
    2. If unsure, try to reseat the connection by unplugging and plugging back the cable to the system unit.
  3. Perform power reset
    1. Unplug the system from power
    2. Press and hold the power button for 15–20 seconds, then reconnect the cable and try turning it on again. This removes residual electrical charge that may prevent startup.[6][5]
  4. Inspect internal power connections
    1. Open the case and ensure that the 24-pin motherboard connector and CPU power connector are fully inserted.
    2. Remove and reconnect them if necessary.[4]
  5. Check the motherboard
    1. Try to turn on the system and observe if there are any LED indicators or listen for beep codes. These signals help identify whether the motherboard is receiving power and where the failure occurs.[5][2]
  6. Test PSU - Before performing this test, understand that a PSU may turn on but still be faulty if it delivers incorrect voltage. A multimeter allows you to measure the actual electrical output and verify if it meets standard values required by the system.[4]
    1. Turn off and unplug the computer to prevent electrical shock while handling internal components.
    2. Open the system unit case
    3. Locate the 24-pin motherboard connector. It is the largest cable from the PSU, which supplies multiple voltage lines to the motherboard. Unplug it from the motherboard.
    4. Other cables connected from the PSU to the motherboard must also be unplugged. This may include the CPU power cable, GPU power cable and the SATA power cables (drives).
    5. Detach the power supply by unscrewing it from the system unit case.
    6. Plug the PSU back into an outlet and set the multimeter to DC voltage (V⎓), typically at the 20V range for accurate low-voltage measurement.[4][7]
    7. Place the black probe into any pin connected to a black wire (ground) to complete the measurement circuit.[4]
    8. Use the red probe to test different wires: yellow wire should read about +12V, red wire about +5V, and orange wire about +3.3V; insert the probe carefully into the back of the connector where the metal contacts are exposed.[4][7]
    9. Check if values fall within acceptable limits: 12V (11.4–12.6V), 5V (4.75–5.25V), and 3.3V (3.14–3.47V); values outside these ranges indicate a faulty PSU.[4][7]
    10. Once measurements are complete, disconnect the PSU from power and close the system unit safely.[7]

b. Computer Turns On but No Display

A computer may be able to turn on, however you may not be able to see anything from the monitor. During such cases, this may indicate that there is an issue with the display output, as well as problems in critical components such as RAM or the GPU. Faulty RAM can prevent proper system initialization, which may lead to data corruption if the system attempts to operate with unstable memory. Similarly, GPU-related issues may signal overheating or hardware degradation. Ignoring this issue may result in further instability, potential hardware damage, or complete system failure over time.[4][6]

sections.title: This issue is likely present if the following symptoms were observed

sections.content:
1. Fans spinning – shows that the system is receiving power and partially functioning.[6]
  2. Lights are on – indicates that internal components are active.
  3. Blank or black screen – means no video signal is being output or received.

If all of these symptoms check out, a display output issue is very likely. This happens due to the following reasons; all will then be checked during our troubleshooting process:

  • Faulty monitor – prevents display even if the system is working.
  • Loose display cable – interrupts signal transmission between system and monitor.
  • Incorrect input source – causes the monitor to look at the wrong signal channel.
  • Faulty RAM – prevents the system from completing the boot process properly.
  • GPU failure – when having a GPU support display, it may stop the system from generating video output.

For a computer that does not show any display, we will be going through the following troubleshooting steps:

  1. Check monitor power
    1. Ensure the monitor is plugged in, powered on, and showing indicator lights.
    2. If not, try to test the outlet by using another device to confirm if there is electricity works.[6]
  2. Reconnect cables
    1. Unplug both ends of the cable. Check for bent pins or damage and replace the cable if needed.[6]
    2. Reconnect the cables on the monitor and display output from the system unit.
    3. Try to use other display output ports to see if it works.
  3. Select correct input
    1. Press the monitor’s menu button.
    2. Navigate to input/source settings.
    3. Select the correct port based on the connection type (HDMI, VGA, DP).
    4. Cycle through options if unsure. This ensures the monitor is receiving the correct signal.[6]
  4. Reseat RAM.
    1. Turn off and unplug the system.
    2. Remove the graphics card from its slot.
    3. Reinsert it firmly and reconnect power cables.
    4. If available, connect the monitor to the motherboard video port (integrated graphics).
      This determines whether the GPU is faulty.[4][2]
  5. Test GPU - If the computer you are working on utilizes a graphics card for display output
    1. Turn off and unplug the system.
    2. Remove the graphics card from its slot.
    3. Reinsert it firmly and reconnect power cables.
    4. If available, connect the monitor to the motherboard video port (integrated graphics). This determines whether the GPU is faulty.[4][2]

c. Computer Restarting Randomly

When a computer automatically restarts without your control, it places stress on both hardware and software components. Sudden shutdowns can interrupt processes and lead to data corruption. Additionally, repeated power cycling can strain components such as the PSU and motherboard. If the cause is overheating, continued operation under high temperatures can degrade internal components, shortening their lifespan significantly.[4][8]

sections.title: The following symptoms are observed when experiencing this issue

sections.content:
1. Sudden restart – indicates unstable system operation.[7]
  2. Blue screen errors – shows critical system failure.
  3. Restart loops – suggests unresolved hardware or power issues.

There are several reasons why this issue happens. Most of the time, it is due to the following situations regarding hardware components:

  • Overheating – When internal temperatures rise too high, the system may restart automatically to protect the processor and other components from damage. [8].
  • Faulty RAM – Unstable or poorly seated memory can interrupt processing and force the computer to crash or restart. [4][2]
  • PSU instability – If the power supply cannot deliver steady voltage, the system may restart unexpectedly under load. [7][2]
  • Motherboard issues – Damaged circuits, failing slots, or unstable power regulation on the board can trigger restart. [2]

While this issue is important to address immediately to prevent further complications to the components, we must first begin with the least invasive and most common causes.

  1. Check temperature of the CPU
    1. Restart the computer and enter BIOS or UEFI by pressing the required key during startup, usually Delete, F2, or F10 depending on the motherboard. You can look up how to access it through the manufacturer’s manual of the motherboard, through the boot sequence displayed when booting the computer, or online.
    2. Look for a section labeled Hardware Monitor, PC Health, or a similar name, then read the CPU temperature.
    3. If the temperature is already unusually high while the system is doing very little, overheating is likely involved. [8]
  2. Clean cooling system
    1. Shut down the computer, unplug it, open the case, and use compressed air, or a soft brush to remove dust from the CPU fan, heat sink fins, case fans, and air vents.
    2. Hold the fan blades in place while cleaning so they do not spin excessively from the air pressure. Dust buildup restricts airflow and prevents heat from being removed efficiently, so cleaning can reduce temperature and improve stability. [8]
    3. With the case open and after making sure nothing is loose inside, power the computer on and visually confirm that the CPU fan and case fans are spinning normally. If a fan is not spinning, spins unevenly, or makes unusual noise, the system may not be cooling properly.[8]
  3. Test RAM
    1. Shut the system down, unplug it, open the case, and locate the RAM modules.
    2. Press the side clips outward, remove the modules carefully, then reinstall one module firmly into the recommended slot until both clips lock.
    3. Start the computer and test stability with one stick at a time. If the problem disappears with one module but returns with another, one stick may be faulty. This process helps determine whether unstable memory is causing the restarts. [2][4]
  4. Test PSU
    1. If temperatures and RAM appear normal, test the PSU using a multimeter procedure or temporarily substitute a known-good PSU with sufficient wattage for the system.
    2. If random restarts stop after using a different PSU, unstable power delivery is likely the cause. This helps confirm whether the system is restarting because voltage output becomes unreliable, especially under load. [2][7]

d. Computer Overheating

Overheating happens when the computer generates more heat than its cooling system can remove. A user may notice the system unit case becoming hot to the touch, the fans running loudly, reduced performance, or sudden shutdowns. This issue is serious because all computer components operate best within safe temperature ranges, and once those ranges are exceeded, the system begins to protect itself by slowing down or shutting off. In more serious cases, prolonged overheating can permanently damage sensitive parts. [8]

sections.title: To confirm overheating, the following signs can be observed

sections.content:
1. Hot case or exhaust air – the air coming from the system may feel unusually warm, suggesting poor heat removal.[8]
  2. Loud fans – the cooling system is working harder than normal to reduce temperature.[8]
  3. Slow performance – the processor may reduce speed to protect itself, a behavior commonly called thermal throttling. [8]
  4. Sudden shutdown – the system may power off to prevent heat damage. [8]

Computers typically face this issue whenever there is an issue with temperature regulation. As such, it can be caused by the following:

  • Dust buildup – Dust blocks vents and heat sink fins, reducing airflow and trapping heat inside the system. [8]
  • Poor airflow – Blocked vents, cramped placement, or poor internal cable arrangement can prevent heat from leaving the case efficiently. [8]
  • Faulty or obstructed fans – A fan that does not spin properly cannot move enough air through the system, specially the CPU fan. [8]
  • Old or poorly applied thermal paste – If heat is not transferred effectively from the CPU to the heat sink, temperatures rise quickly. [8]

The best approach is to confirm the overheating condition first by following these steps:

  1. Check system temperature
    1. Restart the computer, enter BIOS or UEFI, and look for the CPU temperature reading in the monitoring section.
    2. If available, also check system fan speeds. A high idle temperature suggests the cooling system is not working efficiently even when the computer is not under heavy load. This confirms that the problem is thermal rather than purely software-related. [8]
  2. Clean fans, vents, and heat sinks
    1. Shut down and unplug the computer, open the case, and use compressed air to blow dust out of the CPU cooler, case fans, rear and front vents, and heat sink fins.
    2. Hold the fan blades steady while cleaning. This helps remove airflow restrictions so cooler air can move through the case and hot air can escape. [8]
  3. Check for fan blockage or failure
    1. Inspect whether any cables are touching fan blades and confirm that all fans spin properly when the system is on.
    2. If a fan is not spinning or spins weakly, it may need replacement. [8]
  4. Improve case airflow
    1. Move cables away from airflow paths.
    2. Make sure the computer is not pushed tightly against a wall.
    3. Ensure the vents are not blocked by dust, cloth, or nearby objects. Good airflow allows cool air to enter and warm air to leave, reducing internal heat buildup. [8]
  5. Reapply thermal paste if needed
    1. If overheating continues after cleaning and airflow checks, power down the system, remove the CPU cooler, and clean off the old thermal paste using isopropyl alcohol and a lint-free cloth.
    2. Apply a small pea-sized amount of new thermal paste to the CPU, and reinstall the cooler evenly. This improves heat transfer from the CPU into the cooler, which can significantly lower temperature if the old paste has dried out or been applied poorly. [8]

e. Hard Drive Not Detected

When a hard drive is not detected, the computer cannot properly communicate with the storage device. To the user, this may appear as a missing drive in BIOS, a “No boot device found” message, or an operating system that cannot load. This issue can be caused by loose cables, disabled BIOS ports, drive failure, or communication problems between the motherboard and storage device. [9]

sections.title: An undetectable hard drive issue can be observed through the following symptoms

sections.content:
1. Drive does not appear in BIOS – the system firmware cannot see the drive at startup. [9]
  2. “No boot device” or similar startup error – the computer cannot find a storage device with a usable operating system. [9]
  3. Operating system loads but a secondary drive is missing – the system starts, but one storage device does not appear in the file system or disk tools. [9]
  4. Drive appears intermittently – the storage device may show up sometimes and disappear later, suggesting a loose connection or developing hardware fault. [9]

In this case, several conditions can prevent the motherboard and drive from communicating correctly, including:

  • Loose SATA data cable – Interruption with the communication path between the drive and motherboard is present. [9]
  • Loose SATA power cable – It prevents the drive from receiving proper power. [9]
  • Disabled BIOS port or storage setting – Some systems may have unused ports disabled in BIOS by default. [9]
  • Faulty drive – Internal mechanical or electronic failure can stop the drive from being detected. [9]

The most important parts to inspect are those involved in storage power and communication, where the best process is to confirm the simplest possible causes first, such as cable and BIOS issues, before assuming the drive itself has failed. [9]

  1. Reconnect the SATA data and power cables
    1. Shut down and unplug the computer, open the case, and locate the affected drive.
    2. Remove then firmly reconnect both the SATA data cable and SATA power cable.
    3. Check the motherboard end of the SATA data cable. This helps rule out loose or partially connected cables, which are a common cause of drive non-detection. [9]
  2. Try another SATA port or cable
    1. If the drive is still not detected, move the SATA data cable to a different motherboard SATA port and, if possible, use a different known-good SATA cable. This helps determine whether the problem is with the cable or the original motherboard port rather than the drive itself. [9]
  3. Check BIOS detection
    1. Restart the computer and enter BIOS or UEFI
    2. Open the storage, SATA, or boot section to see whether the drive appears in the device list.
    3. If the port is disabled, enable it if the BIOS allows. This helps confirm whether the motherboard can see the drive before the operating system even loads. [9]
  4. Test the drive in another system or enclosure
    1. If the drive still is not detected, connect it to another compatible computer or an external SATA-to-USB adapter or enclosure.
    2. If the other system also cannot detect it, the drive itself is more likely to be defective. This helps separate drive failure from motherboard or cable issues in the original computer. [9]

f. Peripherals Not Working

Peripherals are external devices that let the user interact with the computer or receive output from it, such as the keyboard, mouse, headphones or speakers, second monitor, and printer. When a peripheral stops working, the problem is not always physical right away; in many cases, the issue begins with Windows settings, device selection, detection, or services. Because of that, troubleshooting should begin with system-level checks first, then move to connection and hardware checks if the issue remains. [2][4][6]

sections.title: Keyboard

sections.content:
Signs that suggest a keyboard issue may be caused either by Windows failing to recognize the keyboard correctly or by a problem with the keyboard’s connection or hardware.

Keyboard types nothing at all – Windows may not be recognizing the device, the USB connection may be unstable, or the keyboard may not be receiving power. [2][4]

Some keys do not respond – debris, stuck keys, or partial hardware failure may be affecting specific keys only. [2][4]

Keyboard works sometimes, then stops – the USB port, cable, or device connection may be loose or intermittent. [2][4]

Because the keyboard depends on both system recognition and physical connection, troubleshooting should begin by confirming that the device is being detected properly before assuming it is damaged. [2][4]

sections.title: Check whether Windows recognizes the keyboard

sections.content:
Press the Windows key + I to open Settings.

Go to Bluetooth & devices, and confirm whether the keyboard is active and responding.

If the keyboard is not functioning well enough to do this, use the mouse to open Settings from the Start menu instead. This step helps determine whether Windows is detecting the device at all before moving to hardware checks. [2]

sections.title: Remove and reconnect the keyboard directly to the PC

sections.content: Unplug the keyboard from the current USB port, wait a few seconds, then plug it back into a USB port directly on the computer instead of through a USB hub. This matters because Microsoft recommends checking whether the device is connected properly and whether a hub is interfering with detection or power. [2]

sections.title: Try another USB port

sections.content: Unplug the keyboard and move it to a different working USB port on the same computer. If the keyboard works in the new port, the original port may be faulty rather than the keyboard itself. [2]

sections.title: Inspect the cable and connector

sections.content:
Check the keyboard cable for cuts, fraying, severe bends, or looseness near the USB plug.

Inspect the connector itself for visible damage. This helps rule out a physical connection problem that could interrupt communication with the computer. [2][4]

sections.title: Test the keyboard on another computer

sections.content:
Connect the same keyboard to another working computer. If it also fails there, the keyboard itself is likely defective.

If it works on another computer, the problem is more likely on the original system. [2]

sections.title: Check for stuck or physically obstructed keys

sections.content: Examine the keys closely and press any key that looks stuck. If needed, gently clean visible debris around the keys. This is especially important if only some keys are failing rather than the whole keyboard. [2][4]

sections.title: Mouse

sections.content:
Mouse movement and clicking depend on both software recognition and hardware input. [2][4]

Cursor does not move – the mouse may not be detected, may not have power, or may be disconnected. [2][4]

Cursor moves erratically – the sensor may be dirty, the surface may be unsuitable, or the connection may be unstable. [2][4]

Left or right click does not register properly – the internal switch may be worn or partially failing. [2][4]

By checking Windows recognition, USB ports, sensor condition, and behavior on another computer, we can identify whether the issue is in the mouse or in the original system. [2][4]

sections.title: Confirm the mouse is connected and active in Windows

sections.content:
Open Settings using Windows key + I.

Go to Bluetooth & devices and check whether the mouse is connected and usable. This helps verify whether Windows is recognizing the mouse before assuming the hardware is defective. [2]

sections.title: Reconnect the mouse directly to the computer

sections.content: Unplug the mouse, wait a few seconds, then reconnect it to a direct USB port on the PC instead of through a hub. Microsoft specifically recommends reconnecting devices and avoiding an unpowered hub when diagnosing mouse problems. [2]

sections.title: Try a different USB port

sections.content: Move the mouse to another working USB port and test whether the cursor responds. If it works in another port, the original port may be the real source of the issue. [2]

sections.title: Clean the mouse sensor and check the surface

sections.content:
Turn the mouse over and inspect the optical sensor area.

Gently remove dust or debris, then test the mouse on a flat, non-reflective surface. This helps rule out tracking problems caused by dirt or an unsuitable surface rather than by hardware failure. [2][4]

sections.title: Inspect the cable or connector

sections.content: If the mouse is wired, check the cable for visible damage or looseness near the connector. If the connection is unstable, cursor movement and clicks may stop intermittently. [2][4]

sections.title: Test the mouse on another computer

sections.content: Connect it to another known working system. If the same problem happens there, the mouse itself is likely faulty. [2]

sections.title: Headphones or Speakers

sections.content:
Sound issues are often caused by misconfigurations. These symptoms rule out this possibility:

No sound at all – Windows may be sending audio to the wrong output device, the device may not be connected properly, or the port may not be working. [2][5]

Sound is very low or distorted – the wrong output format or device settings may be selected, or the cable or speakers may be damaged. [1][5]

Sound only comes from one side – the cable, jack, or speaker driver may be partially failing. [4][5]

Windows can store multiple output devices and may switch between them automatically, the first step should always be to verify audio settings before moving on to physical checks. [1][5]

sections.title: Check the selected output device in Windows

sections.content:
Click the speaker icon on the taskbar and verify that the correct headphones or speakers are selected.

You can also go to Start > Settings > System > Sound, then under Output, choose the correct audio device. This is important because Microsoft notes that sound may fail simply because Windows is sending audio to the wrong output. [1][5]
Set the correct default playback device

Go to Start > Settings > System > Sound, then under Related settings, open the Sound Control Panel.

On the Playback tab, select the correct speakers or headphones and choose Set Default. This ensures that Windows uses the intended device for audio output. [5]

sections.title: Check volume and mute settings

sections.content:
In Start > Settings > System > Sound, make sure the output volume is not muted and the slider is turned up.

Check any inline volume controls on speakers or headphones if present. This step rules out simple volume misconfiguration before deeper troubleshooting. [1][5]

sections.title: Restart Windows audio services if sound remains absent or unstable

sections.content:
Select Start, type services.msc, and press Enter.

In the Services window, locate Windows Audio, right-click it, and choose Restart.

Do the same for Windows Audio Endpoint Builder. Microsoft recommends restarting these services when audio becomes unstable or stops unexpectedly. [12]

sections.title: Check the physical audio connection

sections.content:
Make sure the headphone plug or speaker cable is inserted fully into the correct audio-out port.

If the system has multiple 3.5 mm jacks, verify that the device is plugged into the proper output jack rather than a microphone or line-in jack. [1]

sections.title: Try another port or another device

sections.content:
Connect the speakers or headphones to a different compatible port or test them on another computer or phone.

If they work elsewhere, the issue is more likely on the original PC. [1][5]

sections.title: Inspect the cable and connector

sections.content:
Check for cuts, bent connectors, or a loose plug.

If sound only comes from one side, this is especially important because partial cable failure often causes one-channel audio loss. [4][5]

sections.title: Second Monitor

sections.content:
Like problems with the earlier discussed devices, main and secondary monitor issues are likely caused by configuration errors or device damage.

Second monitor shows no image – Windows may not be set to extend or duplicate the display, the monitor may not be detected, or the cable may not be carrying a signal. [2][6]

“No Signal” appears on the monitor – the monitor may be on the wrong input source or the cable may be disconnected or faulty. [2][6]

Monitor is connected but not listed in display settings – Windows may not have detected it yet. [2][6]

Windows may be set to show output only on the main screen, or the monitor may simply be listening to the wrong input source. Because of that, the system display settings should be checked before replacing cables or suspecting the monitor itself. Go through these steps to further isolate the issue: [2][6]

sections.title: Check the Windows display mode first

sections.content:
Press Windows key + P and make sure Extend is selected if you want the second monitor to act as an additional screen.

If the PC screen only is selected, the second display may remain blank even though it is connected. Microsoft specifically recommends this as an early step when troubleshooting external monitor connections. [2][6]

sections.title: Detect the monitor in Settings

sections.content:
Go to Start > Settings > System > Display.

Scroll to Multiple displays, then select Detect. This tells Windows to search again for an attached monitor that did not appear automatically. [6]

sections.title: Confirm the monitor’s input source

sections.content:
Turn on the monitor, open its built-in menu using the monitor buttons, and select the correct input such as HDMI, DisplayPort, or VGA, depending on the cable being used.

If the wrong input is selected, the monitor can appear dead even when the connection is otherwise fine. [2][6]

sections.title: Reconnect the display cable

sections.content: Turn off both the computer and the monitor, unplug the video cable from both ends, inspect it for bent pins or visible damage, and reconnect it firmly.

sections.title: This helps restore signal transmission and rules out a loose connection. [2][6]
Try another cable or another video port

sections.content:
If the monitor is still not detected, replace the cable with a known working one or move it to a different video output port on the computer if one is available.

This helps identify whether the problem is in the cable or port rather than in the monitor itself. [[2][6]

sections.title: Test the second monitor with another system

sections.content:
Connect the monitor to another working computer.

If it works there, the original system’s output path or configuration is more likely at fault. [2][6]

sections.title: Printer

sections.content:
A printer can be powered on and connected physically, yet still fail to print because Windows is sending the job to the wrong printer, the correct printer is marked offline, or the job queue is stuck.

Printer does not print at all – Windows may not be using the correct printer, the printer may be offline, or the connection may have failed. [2][10]

Print jobs stay queued but do not print – the printer may be paused, offline, or unable to communicate properly with the computer. [2][10]

Error lights appear on the printer – the printer itself may have a hardware or readiness issue even before the computer sends a job successfully. [2][4]

First checks should be done inside Windows before moving to cables and ports. [2][10]

sections.title: Check the selected printer in Windows

sections.content:
Go to Start > Settings > Bluetooth & devices > Printers & scanners, then confirm that the intended printer appears in the list.

If multiple printers are installed, make sure you are sending the job to the correct one. This helps rule out wrong-device selection, which is a common reason print jobs seem to disappear. [10]

sections.title: Open the printer queue and check status

sections.content:
In Printers & scanners, select the printer, then open its queue.

Make sure the printer is not set to Use Printer Offline and that jobs are not stuck in a paused state.

If needed, clear stuck jobs from the queue. This helps restore normal communication between Windows and the printer. [10]

sections.title: Restart the printer

sections.content:
Turn the printer off, wait a few seconds, then turn it back on.

This resets the printer’s internal state and can clear temporary faults that prevent it from accepting jobs. [2][10]

sections.title: Check the physical connection

sections.content:
If the printer uses USB, unplug and reconnect the USB cable securely on both the printer and the computer.

If possible, avoid USB hubs and connect directly to the PC. This helps ensure that communication is not being interrupted by a weak or unstable path. [2][4]

sections.title: Try another port or cable

sections.content:
Connect the printer using a different USB port or another known working cable.

If the printer responds on the new port or cable, the original connection path was likely faulty. [2][4]

sections.title: Test the printer on another computer

sections.content: If available, connect the same printer to another working system. If it still fails there, the problem is more likely with the printer itself. [2][4]

sections.title: Topic 4: Software Issues

sections.content:
Software is the set of instructions or programs that tell a computer what to do. It is separate from hardware, which refers to the physical components of the computer, and it includes the operating system, applications, utilities, and other digital tools that make the system functional and programmable. Without software, the hardware would not know how to perform tasks, display information, or respond to user input. [22]

Problems that affect the programs, operating system functions, and digital instructions that allow the computer to work properly can be classified as software issues. Unlike hardware issues, these problems do not usually involve broken physical parts at first; instead, they often involve corrupted files, failed updates, incorrect settings, driver problems, malware, or applications that do not behave as expected. Because software controls how hardware is used, software issues can still cause serious effects on performance, stability, and usability even when the physical components are still working. [22][23][24]

sections.title: Software issues commonly happen because of the following

sections.content:
Frequent updates and changes – operating systems, apps, and drivers are updated regularly, and incomplete or failed updates can cause problems. [23][24]

Incorrect settings or misconfiguration – software may stop working properly if the wrong device, option, or system setting is selected. [23][25]

Corrupted system or application files – damaged files can prevent Windows features or apps from working as intended. [23][25]

Too many background processes or startup apps – these can slow the system down or cause instability over time. [24]

Malware or unwanted software – harmful or unwanted programs can affect performance, security, and normal system behavior. [24]

In CHS, resolving software issues means applying a structured troubleshooting process to identify what part of the operating system, application, driver, or configuration is causing the problem. It involves understanding how software controls the system, how misconfiguration or corrupted files can imitate hardware problems, and how proper troubleshooting helps the technician solve issues more accurately and efficiently. [23][25]

questions.question: Why are software issues common?

sections.title: Topic 5: Common Software Issues

sections.content:
a. Operating System Not Booting

When the operating system does not boot, the computer may power on but fail before reaching the desktop. In many cases, the machine gets stuck on a manufacturer logo, shows an automatic repair loop, or displays recovery-related messages instead of loading Windows normally. This usually means that Windows startup files, boot configuration data, update state, or disk-related system files are damaged, incomplete, or inaccessible. [11][13][21]

Before listing specific causes, it is important to recognize what “not booting” looks like in practice. The system may still receive power and may even pass the basic hardware startup phase, but Windows itself does not complete loading. These symptoms help distinguish an operating system boot issue from a total hardware power failure. [11][21]

Stuck on the manufacturer logo or spinning dots for an unusually long time – Windows has begun startup but cannot continue normally. [11][21]

Automatic Repair or recovery screens appear repeatedly – Windows detects a startup problem and keeps redirecting to recovery tools instead of completing boot. [11]

Error messages related to boot files or startup repair appear – the boot configuration, startup files, or recovery path may be damaged. [11][21]

A recent failed update or sudden shutdown happened before the problem started – incomplete updates or abrupt shutdowns can leave startup files in an inconsistent state. [13][21]

A Windows system may fail to boot for several reasons, and many of them affect startup-critical files or settings. Understanding these reasons helps guide troubleshooting in the right order. [11][13][21]

Corrupted or missing system files – Windows needs intact protected system files to load correctly, and corruption can block startup. [13]

Damaged boot configuration or boot records – The system may not know where or how to start Windows properly. [11][21]

Failed or incomplete update – Updates that do not finish correctly can leave Windows in an unstable startup state. [11][21]

Disk-related errors on the system drive – If the storage device has file-system issues, Windows may fail to read startup components correctly. [13][21]

These causes all affect the startup path in different ways, thus the investigation should focus on the storage device that contains Windows, the Windows recovery tools, and the startup configuration itself. If any one of following areas is damaged or misconfigured, the operating system may fail to load even when the rest of the computer appears to be working: [11][13][21]

Windows Recovery Environment (WinRE) – this provides repair tools such as Startup Repair and Command Prompt. [11]

Boot configuration and boot records – these tell the system how to locate and start Windows. [21]

System files on the Windows drive – these are required for Windows to load correctly. [13]

The system drive itself – if the drive has file-system issues, Windows startup can fail even if the hardware powers on normally. [13][21]

The goal is to repair startup behavior without immediately reinstalling Windows, because Microsoft provides built-in recovery methods designed specifically for boot failures. [11][21]

sections.title: Run Startup Repair

sections.content:
Turn the computer on and enter the Windows Recovery Environment.

From there, select Troubleshoot > Advanced options > Startup Repair > Restart.

Startup Repair is designed to automatically look for common startup issues and attempt to fix them without requiring manual repair commands. [11]

sections.title: Try boot-related repair commands in WinRE if Startup Repair does not work

sections.content:
Enter WinRE again, choose Troubleshoot > Advanced options > Command Prompt,.

Run the boot repair sequence appropriate to your system, such as scanning for installed systems and rebuilding startup data. Microsoft’s startup-issue troubleshooting guidance includes repairing boot code and fixing BCD-related errors when automatic repair is not enough. [21]

sections.title: Check and repair system files

sections.content:
If you can reach Command Prompt through recovery, run the supported system-file repair path.

Microsoft documents System File Checker as the Windows tool used to check protected system files for corruption and repair problems that can cause Windows to malfunction.

sections.title: If corruption is involved, repairing those files can restore boot stability. [13]
Use recovery options if startup still fails

sections.content:
If the boot issue continues after repair attempts, use the Windows recovery options that best match the situation, such as System Restore or recovery-based repair paths.

Microsoft specifically recommends choosing the recovery option that most closely matches the scenario when basic startup troubleshooting does not solve the issue. [11][14]

b. Slow Computer

A slow computer usually does not fail all at once; instead, it gradually becomes less responsive. Opening programs takes longer, switching windows feels delayed, startup may take too long, and simple tasks may begin to feel unusually heavy. In many cases, the slow performance is caused by too many background processes, heavy startup apps, low free storage, malware, outdated software, or driver-related inefficiency rather than a single dramatic failure. [12][16]

Recognizing these signs helps determine whether the issue is truly general performance degradation and not just one misbehaving app:

Programs open slowly – the system takes longer than usual to launch software, which suggests reduced overall responsiveness. [12]

Startup takes too long – too many startup items or heavy background processes may be delaying boot into the desktop. [12]

The system responds slowly to clicks or commands – resource usage may be too high, or Windows may be spending too much time handling background activity. [12]

Performance becomes worse over time rather than all at once – this often suggests accumulated software load, storage pressure, or maintenance-related issues rather than immediate hardware failure. [12]

A computer can become slow because the operating system is spending too many resources on tasks that are not helping the user’s current work. Because slowness has many possible causes, it is important to investigate system-level conditions first. [12]

Too many startup or background apps – These consume memory, CPU time, and disk activity even before the user opens anything important. [12]

Malware or unwanted software – Malicious or unwanted processes can consume CPU, memory, and storage resources. [12]

Pending or missing updates and outdated drivers – Windows and hardware can run inefficiently if software maintenance is behind. [12][16]

Storage pressure or system-file issues – When the drive is heavily burdened or core files are damaged, overall system responsiveness can fall. [12][13]

For a slow computer, the focus should be on the operating system’s workload and the parts that affect responsiveness because slowness often comes from accumulated software load, troubleshooting should start with maintenance and configuration inside Windows.[12]

sections.title: Check for Windows updates

sections.content:
Open Start > Settings > Windows Update.

Install available updates, then restart the computer. Microsoft lists Windows updates and optional driver updates among the recommended ways to improve PC performance. [12]

sections.title: Run a malware scan

sections.content:
Go to Start > Settings > Privacy & security > Windows Security > Virus & threat protection.

Select Quick scan. Microsoft notes that malware can consume CPU and disk resources and reduce system performance. [12]

sections.title: Reduce startup load

sections.content:
Open Task Manager, then review startup-related load.

Disable unnecessary startup apps, but be careful with selecting unfamiliar tasks as it may affect the current operation of the computer. You can also disable applications that you are not using and end their activity by right-clicking on the task and choosing ‘’End Task”. This helps reduce the amount of work Windows must do each time it starts and can improve overall responsiveness. [12]

sections.title: Check for driver updates

sections.content:
Right-click Start, open Device Manager.

Expand the relevant hardware category, right-click the device, and choose Update driver.

It will then search automatically for updated driver software. Microsoft documents this as the standard path for updating hardware drivers through Device Manager. [16]

Check for system-file corruption if the slowdown is accompanied by other unusual Windows behavior

Use the supported System File Checker process documented by Microsoft to scan for and repair protected system-file corruption that may cause Windows to malfunction. [13]

c. Computer Freezing Frequently

A computer that freezes frequently may stop responding in the middle of work. The mouse may stop moving, the keyboard may stop responding, or the system may stay visible on screen but refuse to react to clicks. Freezing often points to a stability issue involving the operating system, drivers, memory pressure, storage stalls, or corrupted system files. [12][13][16]

Frequent freezing is different from general slowness because the system stops responding altogether, even if only temporarily. Looking at the pattern of the freezing helps determine whether the issue is tied to startup, one app, heavy multitasking, or general instability, such as what is indicated below: [12][13][16]

The mouse and keyboard stop responding – the system has stalled instead of merely becoming slow. [12]

Windows remains visible on screen but clicks and typing no longer work – the interface has frozen even though the display is still active. [12][17]

Freezing happens repeatedly during the same types of tasks – the pattern may point to a driver, app, or resource problem rather than random failure. [12][16]

Restart temporarily fixes the problem, but it comes back later – this often suggests an unresolved system-level cause. [12][13]

This issue happens when the system becomes unable to complete normal processing smoothly. Common reasons include outdated drivers, damaged system files, resource exhaustion, and software conflicts. [12][13][16]

Corrupted system files – If protected Windows files are damaged, the operating system may malfunction or become unstable. [13]

Outdated or faulty drivers – Unstable device drivers can cause the system to stop responding during hardware communication. [16][19]

Excessive background load – Heavy startup or background activity can overwhelm the system and make it stop responding. [12]

Problematic or unstable apps – One misbehaving app can contribute to repeated freezing, especially if it runs automatically or frequently. [17][18]

Addressing a freezing issue should focus on maintenance and isolation inside the operating system. This approach helps narrow the problem without immediately making drastic changes. [12][13][16]

sections.title: Restart and observe the pattern

sections.content: After the freeze, restart the computer and pay attention to whether freezing happens only with certain apps, only after startup, or during heavy multitasking. This observation helps guide the next steps more accurately. [12][17]

sections.title: Update Windows and drivers

sections.content:
Install pending Windows updates from Settings > Windows Update.

Review important hardware in Device Manager and use Update driver where needed. Microsoft documents Device Manager as the standard way to update hardware drivers. [12][16]

sections.title: Run System File Checker (SFC)

sections.content:
Click the Start menu, type cmd, then right-click Command Prompt and select Run as administrator. This is required because SFC needs full system access to scan and repair protected files. [13]

In the Command Prompt window, type sfc /scannow and press Enter. This command tells Windows to begin scanning all protected system files for corruption. [13]

    1. The scan may take several minutes depending on system speed and file condition. During this process, Windows compares system files against known correct versions and automatically replaces corrupted ones. It is important not to close the window or interrupt the process. [13]
    2. Once the scan finishes, Windows will display a message indicating whether corrupted files were found and whether they were repaired successfully. This helps determine if system file corruption was contributing to the freezing issue. [13]
    3. After the scan completes, restart the system to apply any repairs made by the system.

sections.title: Check whether a specific app is involved

sections.content:
If freezing consistently happens after opening one program, try repairing that app from Start > Settings > Apps > Installed apps.

Select the app, choose More, and then use the repair option if available. Microsoft states that some apps can be repaired from Settings without reinstalling them. [17]

sections.title: Reduce background pressure

sections.content: Review startup and background activity so Windows is not trying to handle unnecessary load while you troubleshoot. This helps determine whether freezing improves when the system is under less pressure. [12]

d. Applications Crashing

Application crashes happen when a program opens but then closes unexpectedly, stops working, or throws an error and disappears. Sometimes only one app is affected, while other times several apps begin crashing around the same period. An application crash usually has a narrower scope than a full system freeze or blue screen. The operating system may stay running, but the affected app becomes unstable and exits unexpectedly. [13][17][18]

One app closes unexpectedly while Windows keeps working – the problem is likely within the app or its interaction with Windows. [17][18]

The app opens, then immediately fails – the app may be damaged, missing data, or incompatible with current system conditions. [17][18]

Multiple apps start failing around the same time – the issue may involve Windows components, updates, or broader system-file corruption. [13][17]

Store apps show signs of failing specifically through the Microsoft Store ecosystem – account, store state, or store app health may be involved. [18]

Application crashes do not always mean the program itself is badly written. In many cases, the app files are damaged, the app needs repair, the system files behind it are corrupted, or Windows has not been updated properly. Understanding these reasons helps make troubleshooting more targeted and less random. [13][17][18]

Damaged app files or settings – The program may need repair or reset. [17]

Problems specific to Microsoft Store apps – Store sign-in, app installation state, or store-related app issues may be involved. [18]

Corrupted Windows system files – App stability can suffer if core protected files are damaged. [13]
Outdated Windows or driver environment – The app may be running against an unstable or outdated system context. [16][17]

Troubleshooting app crashes should begin with app repair and then move outward to the operating system itself. This is more efficient than reinstalling Windows or changing hardware before testing whether the program can simply be repaired or reset. [17][18]

sections.title: Repair the app from Settings

sections.content:
Open Start > Settings > Apps > Installed apps.

Find the app, select More, and choose the available repair option. Microsoft states that some apps and programs can be repaired from the Settings page without reinstalling them. [17]

sections.title: For Microsoft Store apps, follow Store-specific app fixes

sections.content: Microsoft recommends working through its built-in sequence for Store app problems, including closing and reopening the app and then continuing through the supported repair path. [18]

sections.title: Make sure Windows is updated

sections.content: If app crashes continue, install pending Windows updates because Microsoft recommends updates as part of resolving app-related issues. [17][18]

sections.title: Run System File Checker

sections.content: If multiple apps are crashing or repair does not help, use the supported SFC process to check for protected system-file corruption that may be affecting the application environment. [13]

sections.title: Reinstall the app if repair is not enough

sections.content:
Microsoft’s Store app guidance includes reinstalling apps when earlier fixes do not resolve the problem. This is appropriate after repair attempts have already failed. [18]

e. File Corruption

File corruption happens when a file’s data is altered or damaged so that the system or an application can no longer read it correctly. File corruption may not always look dramatic at first. Sometimes a file simply refuses to open, opens with errors, or behaves differently than expected. In more serious cases, corruption affects Windows system files and starts causing broader operating-system problems. [13]

Files refuse to open or report errors – the contents may be damaged or incomplete. [13]

Windows features or functions start behaving incorrectly – protected system files may be corrupted. [13]

Problems began after an interrupted shutdown, crash, or incomplete update – corruption often appears after write processes are interrupted. [13]

Several parts of Windows begin malfunctioning instead of only one app – this may indicate system-file corruption rather than a single damaged document. [13]

File corruption usually means that data was not written, stored, or maintained correctly. That can happen because of the following: [13]

Interrupted shutdown or restart – Files may not have finished writing properly. [13]

Failed or incomplete update – Updated files may be left in a damaged or inconsistent state. [13]

Corrupted protected system files – Windows itself may be affected, causing broader malfunction. [13]

File-system problems on the drive – The storage structure that tracks files may be affected. [13]

The most important thing to solve this issue is to begin with the built-in repair tools that Microsoft documents for system-file integrity. That approach is safer and more systematic than manually replacing files without knowing whether Windows itself is damaged. [13]

sections.title: Run System File Checker

sections.content: Use the supported Microsoft SFC process to scan protected system files and repair any that are found to be corrupted. It helps check system files for corruption and helps fix problems that may cause Windows to malfunction. [13]

sections.title: Restart after repair and test the affected file or feature again

sections.content: If the corruption affected Windows behavior, restart the machine and verify whether the issue still occurs. This helps confirm whether SFC corrected the underlying problem. [13]

sections.title: If the problem is app-specific, repair the app as well

sections.content:
If only one application’s files seem damaged, use the repair option from Settings > Apps > Installed apps where available. [17]

If corruption appears after recent Windows trouble, make sure Windows is updated and stable

Microsoft’s SFC guidance begins by making sure the latest Windows updates are installed and restarting the machine. [13]

f. Blue Screen of Death

A Blue Screen of Death, or BSOD, happens when Windows encounters a critical error that it cannot safely recover from while the system is running. Instead of continuing in an unstable state, Windows stops and displays a stop code error screen before restarting or waiting for user action. This type of failure is more severe than an ordinary app crash because it affects the entire operating system and usually involves drivers, low-level software, updates, or hardware interaction. [14]

A BSOD is usually easy to recognize because it interrupts the entire system. However, the details around it still matter because they help identify whether the problem is new hardware, a driver issue, or a repeated stop code. These problem is indicated through the following: [14]

A blue error screen with a stop code appears – Windows has encountered a critical condition it could not safely continue through. [14]

The system restarts unexpectedly after the stop screen – Windows may reboot after the crash depending on settings and severity. [14]

The same stop code appears more than once – repeated codes suggest a repeatable root cause rather than a one-time glitch. [14]

The issue started after new hardware, a driver change, or a recent major system change – Microsoft specifically points to newly added hardware and safe mode troubleshooting in its stop-code guidance. [14]

This error usually happens because Windows detects a condition that could damage data or leave the system in an unsafe state. Common triggers include the following: [14]

Newly added hardware – Microsoft explicitly recommends removing new hardware if the blue screen started after it was installed. [14]

Faulty or incompatible drivers – Low-level driver failures can trigger stop code errors. [14][16][19]

Unstable or corrupted Windows state – Updates, recovery problems, or system corruption can contribute to stop errors. [13][14]

A broader system issue severe enough to require recovery or restore – Microsoft includes restore and recovery options when basic steps do not resolve repeated stop-code errors. [14]

BSOD troubleshooting should start with the most recent changes and the least invasive recovery paths first. [14]

sections.title: Remove any newly added hardware

sections.content: If the blue screen started after a hardware was added, shut down the PC, remove that hardware, and restart.

sections.title: Start in safe mode if normal startup is unreliable

sections.content:
If the system repeatedly crashes, restart it several times to trigger the recovery environment automatically.

    1. Once recovery mode appears, select Troubleshoot > Advanced options > Startup Settings.
    2. Click Restart, and after reboot, a list of options will appear.
    3. Press the corresponding key (usually 4 or F4) to start in basic Safe Mode. Safe Mode will load only essential services, which helps isolate the cause of instability.
    4. If the system runs without crashing in Safe Mode, the issue is likely caused by drivers or software that are not loaded in this mode.

sections.title: Update or reinstall drivers for suspect devices

sections.content:
Use Device Manager to update drivers through the supported Microsoft path.

If a device shows a problem in Device Manager, Microsoft’s error-code guidance also documents uninstalling the driver and scanning for hardware changes to reinstall it. [16][19]

sections.title: If the issue continues, use restore or recovery options
Enter Windows Recovery Environment

sections.content:
Select Troubleshoot > Advanced options > System Restore.

Select a restore point from a time when the system was functioning properly. This step is critical because it determines how far back the system will revert.

    1. Follow on-screen instructions to begin restoring system files and settings.
    2. The system will restart after restoration. Do not interrupt this process to avoid further system damage.
    3. After restoration, observe whether the BSOD issue persists. If the issue is resolved, the cause was likely a recent system change or update. ]

g. Devices Not Recognized

A “device not recognized” problem happens when Windows fails to identify hardware correctly after it is connected or after startup. These problems often involve driver issues, USB controller issues, device communication faults, or repeated plug-and-unplug behavior that leaves the port or controller in a bad state. This issue often appears as a recognition failure rather than total hardware absence. Windows may see that something was connected but still fail to initialize it correctly. Additionally, this issue may appear through the following:

Windows reports that the device is not recognized – the device connection is noticed, but initialization fails. [19][20]

The device appears with an error in Device Manager – Microsoft documents many error-code scenarios in Device Manager as driver or detection issues. [19]

The device does not become usable even after being reconnected – the controller, port, or driver may be stuck in an invalid state. [20]

The problem began after repeated insertion and removal – Microsoft notes that USB ports can stop responding correctly after repeated insertions and removals in some cases. [20]

Windows may not have a stable driver path, because the controller is unresponsive, or because the connection path is unstable. Microsoft’s public guidance for Device Manager errors and USB-controller recovery supports these as common directions for troubleshooting. [16][19][20]

Driver corruption or missing drivers – Microsoft’s Device Manager guidance repeatedly points to reinstalling or updating the driver when recognition errors occur. [16][19]

USB controller or port becoming unresponsive – Microsoft documents disabling and re-enabling USB controllers to recover the USB port from an unresponsive condition. [20]

Repeated insertion and removal behavior – This can leave the port or controller in a state where it no longer recognizes the device correctly. [20]

The device is connected but Windows cannot initialize it properly – Device Manager error-code guidance covers cases where drivers are loaded incorrectly or hardware cannot be found properly. [19]

The most useful things to inspect are the driver state, Device Manager status, and the condition of the relevant controller or port. [16][19][20]

sections.title: Check Device Manager first

sections.content: Right-click Start, choose Device Manager, and look for the device under the expected category or under entries with a warning symbol. This helps confirm whether Windows sees the hardware and whether it reports an error condition. [16][19]

sections.title: Update the driver through Device Manager

sections.content:
In Device Manager, right-click the affected device.

Choose Update driver, then Search automatically for updated driver software. Microsoft documents this as the standard way to update a device driver in Windows. [16]

Click Uninstall when prompted. This step clears the corrupted or faulty driver so that Windows can rebuild it.

    1. After uninstalling, restart the system. During startup, Windows will automatically detect the hardware again and attempt to reinstall the appropriate driver. This automatic reinstallation is part of Windows’ built-in hardware detection process.
    2. If the device is not automatically reinstalled after restart, return to Device Manager, click Action on the top menu, then select Scan for hardware changes. This forces Windows to detect and reinstall the device immediately.
    3. Check if the device reappears in Device Manager without warning symbols. This indicates that the driver has been installed correctly and is functioning properly.

sections.title: Reinstall the driver if the update does not help

sections.content:
Open Device Manager.

Look for the device under its category or under Other devices, especially those with a warning symbol (yellow triangle). This indicates that Windows detects the device but cannot properly configure it. [19]

Right-click the device and select “Uninstall device” – this removes the current driver and its configuration from the system. A confirmation prompt will appear to ensure that you intend to remove the device.

sections.title: If the device is USB-based, recover the USB controller path

sections.content:
Open Device Manager.

Scroll down and expand the section labeled Universal Serial Bus controllers. This section lists all USB root hubs, host controllers, and related USB hardware interfaces. [20]

    1. You will typically see multiple entries such as USB Root Hub, Generic USB Hub, or USB Host Controller. Each of these represents part of the USB communication system.
    2. Right-click one of the USB controller entries and select Disable device, then confirm when prompted. This temporarily turns off that controller, effectively stopping communication through it.
    3. Right-click the same device again and select Enable device. This restarts the controller and reinitializes its communication with the system.
    4. Perform the disable/enable process for other USB controllers listed, especially if the issue persists. This ensures all USB communication paths are refreshed.
    5. Unplug the affected device, wait a few seconds, then plug it back in. This allows Windows to attempt recognition again using the refreshed controller. [20]

Check if the device now appears correctly and functions as expected. If successful, the issue was likely due to a temporary controller failure rather than hardware damage.

g. Incorrect Time and Date in BIOS

Incorrect time and date in BIOS usually appears when the computer clock resets after the machine is turned off, unplugged, or left without power for a period of time. Users may notice wrong time at startup, repeated prompts to set date and time, or loss of BIOS settings after shutdown.

This issue is often easy to notice because the clock behavior becomes obviously unreliable.

BIOS date and time are wrong after the computer has been powered off – the clock is not being retained properly. [15]

BIOS settings are lost when the computer is shut down – this suggests the same retention problem affecting configuration memory. [15]

Time-of-day clock stopped or a similar message appears – Dell lists this as a common symptom of CMOS battery failure. [15]

The most common reason for incorrect BIOS time and date is failure of the CMOS coin-cell battery.

Weak or depleted CMOS battery – The system can no longer preserve BIOS time and settings while powered off. [15]

BIOS settings were reset – Time may need to be manually corrected after a reset. [15]

Since this issue is BIOS-level, troubleshooting should begin by confirming the behavior in BIOS.

sections.title: Enter BIOS and check the stored date and time

sections.content:
Restart the computer.

Enter BIOS setup during startup.

Check whether the BIOS time and date are already incorrect before Windows loads. This confirms that the problem exists at the BIOS level and is not only a Windows display issue. [15]

sections.title: Correct the BIOS time and save the settings

sections.content:
Update the date and time in BIOS, save the changes, and restart.

Turn the computer off fully and check later whether the BIOS clock kept the correct value. This helps determine whether the problem is just a wrong setting or a failure to retain that setting. [15]

sections.title: If the problem returns, inspect and replace the CMOS battery

sections.content:
Shut down and unplug the computer.

    1. Open the system unit.
    2. Identify the coin-cell battery mounted on the motherboard, usually in a circular holder.
    3. Gently release the battery from its holder by pushing the retaining clip aside. Avoid using excessive force to prevent damage.
    4. Place a new CMOS battery (commonly CR2032) into the holder with correct polarity (positive side facing up)
    5. Close the case and reconnect the power cable.
    6. Enter BIOS again, set the correct time and date, then save the settings.

ModuleTitle: Pagkukumpuni at Pag-troubleshoot ng mga Kompyuter
Description:
Ipinakikilala ng araling ito sa mga mag-aaral ang mga prinsipyo at kasanayan sa pag-troubleshoot ng mga karaniwang problema sa computer na nakakaapekto sa parehong hardware at software system sa pamamagitan ng isang nakabalangkas na pamamaraan sa pagtukoy ng mga isyu, pagsusuri ng mga ugat na sanhi, at paglalapat ng mga epektibong solusyon upang maibalik ang paggana ng system.


Mga Layunin:

Ang araling ito ay naglalayong:

  1. Tukuyin ang mga karaniwang problema sa hardware ng computer at ang mga indikasyon nito;
  2. suriin at tukuyin ang mga ugat na sanhi ng mga isyu sa hardware gamit ang isang sistematikong pamamaraan sa pag-troubleshoot; at
  3. maglapat ng naaangkop na sunud-sunod na mga pamamaraan sa pag-troubleshoot upang ligtas at epektibong malutas ang mga isyu sa hardware.

sections.title: Paksa 1: Ano ang Pag-troubleshoot?

sections.caption: Lesson 5 Topic 1 Video
sections.content: https://www.dropbox.com/scl/fi/sxgs0i2zficmcqeah3951/Lesson-5-Topic-1.mp4?rlkey=dpaqqy1kfpjmemlx38nldnugs&st=b2cqmdog&dl=0

Ang pag-troubleshoot ay isang sistematikong proseso na ginagamit upang matukoy, masuri, at malutas ang mga problema sa loob ng isang sistema ng computer. Kabilang dito ang pag-obserba ng mga sintomas, paghihiwalay ng mga posibleng sanhi, at paglalapat ng mga solusyon upang maibalik ang normal na operasyon.[1]

Mahalaga ang pag-troubleshoot sa Computer Hardware Servicing (CHS) dahil nagbibigay-daan ito sa mga technician na mahusay na ma-diagnose at maayos ang mga isyung nakakaapekto sa performance ng computer. Kung walang wastong pag-troubleshoot, maaaring lumala ang mga problema, na magdudulot ng pinsala sa hardware, pagkawala ng data, o pagpalya ng system.[2]

Sa araling ito, tatalakayin natin ang maraming isyung karaniwang kinakaharap sa hardware at software ng computer. Matututunan natin kung ano ang mga palatandaan ng mga isyung ito, ano ang mga sanhi nito, at ano ang mga pamamaraan na maaari nating gawin upang i-troubleshoot at ayusin ang mga ito. Kakailanganin ang pamilyaridad sa mga device, ngunit makatitiyak ka na dahil naipasa mo na ang mga nakaraang aralin, mayroon ka nang lubos na kaalaman sa mga pangunahing kaalaman sa CHS at kung paano natin ito ilalapat sa pag-troubleshoot.

Mabilis na Pagtatasa(Hanggang 3 Tanong):
  1. Anong proseso ang ginagamit upang matukoy at maayos ang mga problema sa computer nang paunti-unti?

a. Pag-format
b. Pag-troubleshoot
c. Pag-install
d. Pag-upgrade

sections.title: Paksa 2: Mga Isyu sa Hardware

sections.content:
Ang hardware ay tumutukoy sa mga pisikal na bahagi ng isang sistema ng kompyuter na maaaring makita at mahawakan, tulad ng motherboard, processor, RAM, mga storage device, at mga peripheral.[3]

Karaniwan ang mga isyu sa hardware dahil ang mga pisikal na bahagi ay patuloy na nalalantad sa stress na may kaugnayan sa kapaligiran at paggamit. Sa paglipas ng panahon, ang init, alikabok, at mga pagbabago-bago ng kuryente ay nagpapahina sa mga bahaging ito. Halimbawa, ang isang cooling fan na puno ng alikabok ay hindi na kayang maayos na i-regulate ang temperatura, na humahantong sa sobrang pag-init at pagpalya ng sistema. Bagama't maraming posibleng dahilan para mangyari ang mga isyu sa hardware, maaari nating pagsamahin ang mga ito sa mga sumusunod na dahilan:

  1. Pagkasira at Pagkapunit– ang mga komponent o bahagi ay nasisira sa paglipas ng panahon dahil sa patuloy na paggamit, na kalaunan ay humahantong sa pagkabigo o pagbaba ng kahusayan.
  2. Sobrang pag-init– ang labis na init na dulot ng mahinang bentilasyon o naiipong alikabok ay maaaring makapinsala sa mga panloob na bahagi at paikliin ang kanilang buhay.
  3. Mga Pag-agos ng Kuryente– ang mga biglaang pagtaas ng kuryente ay maaaring makapinsala sa mga sensitibong elektronikong bahagi.
  4. Hindi Wastong Paghawak– ang pisikal na pinsala mula sa pagkahulog o maling pag-install ng mga bahagi ay maaaring magdulot ng malfunction.
  5. Mga Depekto sa Paggawa– maaaring maagang masira ang ilang hardware dahil sa mga depekto sa produksyon.

Sa CHS, ang paglutas ng mga isyu sa hardware ay kinabibilangan ng paggamit ng teknikal na kaalaman, mga kagamitan sa pag-diagnose, at wastong mga pamamaraan upang matukoy at maayos ang mga sirang bahagi. Kabilang dito ang pag-inspeksyon sa mga koneksyon, pagsubok sa mga bahagi, pagpapalit ng sirang hardware, at pagtiyak ng pagiging tugma.[4]

Mabilis na Pagtatasa(Hanggang 3 Tanong):
  1. Ano ang mga isyu sa hardware?

a. Isyu sa software
b. Problema sa hardware
c. Isyu sa network
d. Isyu sa operating system

  1. Bakit karaniwan ang mga problema sa hardware sa mga computer?

a. Katiwalian ng software
b. Pisikal na pagkasira at pagkasira ng mga bahagi
c. Pagkabigo sa internet
d. Problema sa drayber
